data.title: As Copper Prices Soar, Hudbay Minerals Has The Look Of A Winner
data.description:
Back in April, I initiated coverage on Hudbay Minerals (HBM) as a buy, citing the company’s potential for growth and its effort to pay down debt. The stock far exceeded my expectations, as you can see here.

The story of Hudbay’s success tracks most of the mining companies out there. In this case, the price of copper has surged so much that Hudbay’s forward earnings look positively enormous, to the point where even some headwinds that challenged earnings in Q3 and led to a modest miss didn’t impact the stock. The bulls are out in force, and it’s hard to blame them.

We’ll be taking a look at Hudbay as they look today, what happened to them in Q3, and also the status of their various important sites. We’ll also take a look at why the copper market is what it is and whether or not this copper bull market is something we can count on.

Fires And Unrest: Hudbay’s Safe Sites Face Problems

Part of the reason I initially liked Hudbay was that, unlike companies that have mines in seemingly geopolitical “hot zones,” Canada-based Hudbay seemingly has ideal sites. The three major sites included two sites in western Canada and the one in Peru, with a fourth site coming up in Arizona.

Reality quickly showed that safe is a relative term, however. The Constancia site in Peru had some operational interruptions in Q3 because of sporadic social unrest, and the constant wildfires in Manitoba cut back production at the Lalor site about 50%.

Take the underperformance at those two sites and then factor in Copper Mountain in British Columbia coming into the processing of relatively lower-grade ore than normal, and you can see why Hudbay ultimately missed earnings estimates in Q3, despite relatively strong prices.

Weak as Q3 was, 2025 has still been relatively positive for Hudbay, and we’re seeing improved margins in spite of the Q3 slowdown if we look at the full year.

These are strong numbers in and of themselves, but if you factor in the problems Hudbay faced in Q3 and how much higher copper prices are now than they were before, you can see where the company has the potential to make a lot more money than they already are, and considering that, the stock going from $7 per share to around $20 per share might not be unwarranted.

In A Copper Bull Market, Multiples Might Not Be Crazy

Let’s talk numbers here a bit, because when the stock goes up 160%+ in less than a year, sometimes you end up with unsustainably high multiples. But in Hudbay’s case, they were relatively undervalued before, so in some ways it’s not so stark.

(Source: Q3 earnings release 6-K from SEC)

First, we need to note that some of the long-term debt is coming due, so the long-term debt number spilled into the current liabilities, which is why the current ratio looks weaker. At the wrong time this could be an inconvenience for a company, but since Hudbay is trying to pay down their debt, and commodity prices are hitting record highs, this is an ideal time to come up for refinancing or, hopefully, paying off a lot of that debt.

What I really wanted to point out, though, was that the price/book ratio, even after the share price is up so much, is still only 2.56, which is only a little higher than the sector median of 2.27. Since Hudbay has almost no intangibles on their balance sheet, and since the commodity prices are so high, I think it’s tough to argue that they are overpriced on the basis of equity.

Looking at earnings estimates, we also don’t seem to be too high on a forward P/E basis either. The EPS estimate for 2026 is $1.43 right now, and that has yet to be updated to reflect the much higher copper prices, so we could easily expect earnings to be even better. But even taking $1.43, that’s a forward P/E of 13.94, which is neither excessive nor worse than the sector median of 17.84. If anything, I think there’s a reasonable case to be made that Hudbay is somewhat underpriced based on forward earnings, if one believes the copper prices will remain as high as they are now. Which is an important thing to talk about next.

Record Copper On Short Supply And Tariffs, Can It Last?

Copper prices right now seemingly only go one direction: up. Happening amid a persistent bull market in gold and silver, we could be forgiven for thinking such a bull market in copper is inevitable and can only continue. The thing is, we need to look more seriously at what’s happening and why, and how long it can reasonably last.

Supply is a big issue here. Underinvestment in copper mining in the past decade has led to a slow increase in output, and it takes a while to build new copper mines. Recent disruptions at big mines have compounded that shortage in supply, and while that part is very temporary, building up more capacity is more of a medium-term issue and will likely take several years to resolve.

This is a bad time for that, because structural copper demand is rather high right now, and that’s leading to deficits, which will drive the price up like we’ve seen. It’s not unfair to expect that demand to remain generally high, and since it’s going to take some years to get more supply capacity, prices probably will remain relatively high.

That said, there are other factors here. The tariffs in the US are compounding difficulties and driving the prices up even more. Though the current administration is presenting those tariffs as the new normal, the reality of the situation is that it’s anyone’s guess, and one election could easily reverse this entirely, so if the high copper prices were just a tariff thing, I don’t think it’d be great to invest in on that basis.

Moreover, the tariffs are causing macroeconomic problems that could push copper in the other direction. There was talk for months of a global recession being imminent, and if that does happen, we can expect a near-term reduction in copper demand just because of the macro environment.

In the end, I think we’ve got an environment where copper demand should remain relatively high; supply disruptions may resolve themselves, but the underinvestment of the 2010s is still going to take years to work through. I don’t know that I’d project copper going up from here, but I do think it’s fair to believe it will remain on the high side of things and probably not drop back into the lows we saw in 2020.

The good news from that is that Hudbay is in a good position to make quite a bit of money on these higher prices, and they are one of the companies investing in new capacity so that a decade from now, we’ll no longer be shaking our heads about underinvestment.

Copper World: The Arizona Project

We’ve talked about what’s going on with Hudbay’s three major active sites, but also important is that enormous fourth site that they’re in the process of developing, Copper World in Arizona.

Copper World is, once it’s online, going to be staggeringly large. More investment needs to be made in the development of the site, and Hudbay has secured a partner to help pay for it in the form of Mitsubishi, who committed to a contribution of $600 million in return for 30% of Copper World.

When it’s done, the anticipation is that Copper World will be producing up to 100,000 tons of copper per year. That’s the good news, but the bad news is that we’re still several years away, with the commencement of operations not expected until 2029.

At current prices, 100,000 tons of copper would be a game-changer for Hudbay. Even if copper prices soften somewhat, that much new production is still transformative for the company, and while it’s a bit premature to try to estimate the earnings they’ll be making from Copper World, given the size of the operation, it’s safe to say that Copper World will be the largest of all their active sites once it commences.

How It Could All Come Crumbling Down

I’ve been enthusiastic about Hudbay’s best-case scenario so far, but we also need to be realistic that there are ways where things could go bad for them and the stock price might not continue testing new highs.

Obviously the big risk is commodity prices, copper in particular. Though I see it as very unlikely that copper will suddenly take a precipitous fall, if all the recent supply disruptions get resolved and a recession starts to weaken copper demand, it's possible that the price could fall well off these recent highs. Not to the point that Hudbay wouldn’t still be profitable, but enough that we might have to rethink valuation.

The other problem is disruptions on Hudbay’s side of things and the market’s reaction to them. We saw in Q3 that all three of their major active sites underperformed. The market shrugged it off, but if that gets to be a habit, we could find that Hudbay falls out of favor and investors look toward other copper miners to replace them.

Again, I’m not expecting that. Neither the unrest in Peru nor the wildfires in Manitoba are Hudbay’s fault, as such, and even if it does carry over into the next quarter, it’s not really a reflection on them. The market can be fickle, though, and as easily as they shrugged off a miss in Q3, they could either do it again in Q4 or punish them substantially more than the situation warrants.

Hudbay May Yet Have Room To Run

So what’s the bottom line here? In the end, I’m going to reiterate my buy rating. It’s not that I believe the situation is the same now as it was in April, but rather that I should’ve called it a strong buy before and moderated it to a regular buy now as the price went up.

Copper is absolutely on fire with the market, and that sort of exuberance always suggests caution. That said, Hudbay’s multiples aren’t excessive for the situation they’re in, and I believe they still have room to go higher, assuming copper remains strong. We don’t need miracles here, either; Hudbay is just modestly priced for the situation they’re in.

While the copper market right now lends itself to people chasing short-term trades, I think with Hudbay the real story is a few years out. When Copper World finally goes online in a few more years, that’s when I expect the market to really sit up and take notice.

data.title: Wyndham Hotels: Recent Weakness Has Created A Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Shares of Wyndham Hotels & Resorts Inc, Inc (WH) have had a challenging 2025 and have delivered a total return of -20% thus far. Comparably, the S&P 500 has delivered a total return of 17% over the same time period.

Wyndham shares have fallen this year as the company has experienced a challenging operating environment which has resulted in earnings coming in below consensus estimates. I expect recent weakness in the company's underlying business to be short lived and I believe the company's long-term growth prospects remain strong. I view the stock's valuation as highly attractive relative to the broader market and thus rate the stock a Buy.

Business Challenges

2025 has been a challenging year for Wyndham due to weakness in the U.S. hotel market. The company recently lowered its FY 2025 RevPAR guidance to be down 2% - 3% compared to a previous outlook for a decline of 2% to up 1%. At the start of 2025, the company had issued guidance for FY 2025 RevPAR growth of 0.5% - 1% on a constant currency basis. Disappointing RevPAR performance in 2025 has been driven by the U.S. market, which experienced a RevPAR decline of 5% during Q2 2025. International markets have mostly held up better and helped to offset some of the decline in the U.S.

Competitors such as Choice Hotels International (CHH), which operates in a similar segment of the hotel market as Wyndham, have also seen a decline in U.S. RevPAR. For Q3 2025, Choice Hotels reported a 3.2% decline in U.S. RevPAR. I view the fact that the entire industry is seeing a decline the U.S. market as important as it suggests the RevPAR declines that Wyndham is seeing are not specific to Wyndham brands and are not indicative of market share losses. Rather, the decline in Wyndham U.S. RevPAR is due to macro forces such as the government shut down, a focused on reducing government spending, a more challenging U.S. employment market, and lower inbound travel from foreign countries due to a more tense relationship between the U.S. and allies over trade.

Overall, I expect these recent challenges to prove transitory in nature and that the company will be able to return to 2-3% annual RevPAR growth in the U.S. in the medium-term. Moreover, the relatively challenging 2025 U.S. market should set the company up to experience growth in 2026 as comparison levels will be easier. Additionally, it is important to note that despite macro driven challenges in the U.S., the company has still been able to drive growth through other levers in 2025. Net room growth has increased by 4% for the year and overall Adjusted EPS is still expected to increase by ~5% for the year. The fact that Wyndham has still be able to deliver EPS growth despite the challenges in the U.S. market speaks to the robust high quality nature of the company's business. Moreover, the company's capital light business model has allowed it to return $101 million to shareholders, including $70 million in repurchases, thus far in 2025.

Q4 2025 Outlook

Wyndham is set to report Q4 results in early February 2026. Consensus estimates currently call for the company to report revenue of $337.5 million , representing a 1% decline on a year-over-year basis, and a 13.8% drop in Adjusted EPS to $0.90. I view these estimates as reasonable as they align with company guidance. Wyndham has said it expects FY 2025 Adjusted EPS of $4.60 - $4.78 and thus far in 2025 the company has reported to Adjusted EPS of $3.65. Thus, the mid point of company guidance implies Q4 Adjusted EPS of $1.04 per share. Given this, I see an EPS beat as more likely than an EPS miss for Q4.

While I expect the company to deliver a continued decline in U.S. RevPAR during the quarter, I think comments regarding the 2026 outlook will be more important in terms of driving the stock price. Given the relatively weak market in 2025, I think the company should be poised to deliver low single-digit RevPAR growth in 2026. However, management may decide to take a conservative approach to guidance regarding the challenges experienced in 2025. To the extent RevPAR guidance comes in below expectations I would not be overly concerned in the near-term as I would view it as management attempting to be conservative. Additionally, I expect the company to guide towards year-over-year room growth of 4% - 5% which is line with 2025 levels.

Long-Term Growth Story Remains Intact

Wyndham has three key long-term EPS growth drivers: 1) steady addition of new units, 2) RevPAR growth, and 3) significant return of capital through share repurchases. I believe each of these drivers remains in place. As mentioned previously, the company has increased its room count by ~4% in 2025 which was mostly driven by international growth. Moreover, the company also increased its pipeline by 4% this year to a record level of 257,000 rooms. Overall, Wyndham's international room count stands at just 352,000 relative to a U.S. room count of 504,400 and thus I see a significant runway for units growth outside the U.S.

In regards to RevPAR growth, in the long-run I expect this metric to perform inline to slightly better than developed market GDP growth. The reason for this is that I believe travel will continue to be a growth part of the economy with individuals increasingly interested on spending on travel relative to physical goods. Additionally, Wyndham should benefit from an increased share of revenue coming from international markets (due to an increasing share of new units growth) where I see better medium-term RevPAR growth prospects relative to the U.S. market which is more mature. That said, I think RevPAR growth will be less linear than growth driven by new units. 2025 serves as a good example of how RevPAR can experience unexpected downside volatility to the extent economic conditions change.

Wyndham continues to operate a franchise based capital light business model. As of Q3 2025, over 99% of the company's locations were operated under a franchise model. As a result of the attractive fee nature of this business, the company is able to generate 80%+ Adjusted EBITDA margins. Moreover, the lack of capital intensity means that Wyndham is able to use free cash flow to pay dividends and repurchase shares. Over the past five years, as shown by the chart below, the company has used repurchases to fairly steadily decrease its share count by a total of 19% or nearly 4% per year. Given the stock's current valuation of 15.5x forward EPS, I think Wyndham is well positioned to continue repurchasing shares at a similar pace going forward. To the extent the stock were to trade at a much higher valuation multiple then share repurchases would be significantly less accretive to EPS growth.

Overall, on a combined basis, I believe that Wyndham will be able to generate low teens annual EPS growth over the next few years. That said, the growth will likely be lumpy as RevPAR growth may not be linear. My view is roughly inline with consensus estimates which call for the company to deliver EPS growth of 11.3% and 13.8% over the next two years.

Valuation Is Attractive

Wyndham shares currently trade at 15.5x consensus FY 2026 Adjusted EPS. Comparably, the S&P 500 trades at roughly 22x consensus forward earnings. Thus, on a relative basis Wyndham is trading at a substantial discount. I do not believe that this discount is warranted given the fact that Wyndham has low double digit long-term EPS growth potential while the S&P 500 has historically grown earnings at a high single-digit rate historically. While the S&P 500 is expected to generate above trend earnings growth for the next couple years (due to mega cap tech companies with AI exposure) ultimately I think the earnings growth rate for the index will settle back inline with historical norms. In addition to having modestly higher long-term growth prospects than the S&P 500, I also believe that Wyndham's capital light business model argues for a premium valuation on a relative basis. Given these factors, I do not believe Wyndham's significant valuation discount to the broader market is justified.

As shown by the chart below, Wyndham shares are currently trading at a moderate discount relative to their own average historical forward P/E ratio of 17.7x over the past few years. I believe the discount has been driven the weak business performance this year and that the stock is well poised to experience valuation multiple expansion back towards its historical average once RevPAR performance improves.

In addition to trading at an attractive valuation vs the broader market and its own historical averages, I also view Wyndham's valuation relative to peers as fairly attractive. Wyndham trades at a slight premium to Choice Hotels and at a significant discount to Hilton and Marriott. As recently as 2023, Wyndham had been trading close to inline with Hilton and Marriott but Wyndham's valuation multiple has declined while Hilton and Marriott have experienced valuation multiple expansion. One reason for this is the upscale and upper midscale hotel markets (the parts of the market Hilton and Marriott have more exposure to on a relative basis) have performed better than the economy market which Wyndham has more exposure to. Moreover, these peers have been growing earnings faster by increasing their new units growth at a faster rate than Wyndham. For example, Hilton reported Q3 2025 new unit growth of 6.5% while Marriott delivered new unit growth of 4.7%.

Marriott is expected to deliver 12.5% annual EPS growth for the next two years which is similar to Wyndham's expected EPS growth over the same time period and thus I view Wyndham as attractive given the significant valuation discount. Hilton's expected EPS growth rate of 13% and 15% over the same time period justifies some premium but the current degree of difference seems stretched in my view. Choice Hotels has lower near-term growth prospects and is adding new rooms at a much slower pace as its global room count has grown at 2.3% vs last year. Choice Hotels is expected to deliver mid single-digit EPS growth over the next few years and thus I view Wyndham's modest valuation premium as justified.

Overall, I view Wyndham's valuation as highly attractive at current levels and see scope for moderate multiple expansion relative to the broader market and peers. In my view, a likely catalyst for valuation multiple expansion would be a stabilization of U.S. RevPAR and a return to positive RevPAR growth globally.

Risks To Consider

One risk to the Wyndham bull case is that economic conditions significantly worsen and U.S. and International lodging markets weaken. Under such a scenario I would expect RevPAR to decline further (though perhaps less than some peers given Wyndham's limited exposure to the upscale part of the market.) Additionally, I believe that new unit and pipeline growth could slow in the event of a significantly weaker macro environment. Such a scenario is likely to result in Wyndham failing to meet near-term EPS estimates and thus a reset lower in the share price. I do not see a further economic slowdown on the horizon as lower interest rates are likely to help economic conditions in the near-term. Furthermore, I believe the Trump administration will work hard to keep economic conditions as strong as possible ahead of the 2026 election cycle.

If my view is incorrect and we do experience a recession or sharp economic slowdown, I would view any decline in Wyndham shares as a buying opportunity so long as the long-term growth drivers remain in place.

Conclusion

Wyndham shares have underperformed the S&P 500 significantly thus far in 2025 due to weak business performance and valuation multiple contraction. I view recent weakness in U.S. RevPAR as temporary in nature and expect RevPAR growth over the next few years. The company continues to execute on its growth strategy and new unit growth has remained a key earnings growth driver despite a more challenging RevPAR environment.

I believe Wyndham's long-term growth drivers remain in place which should allow the company to deliver low double-digit annual EPS growth in the years ahead. I find the stock's valuation as highly compelling compared to the broader market and attractive vs peers. I rate Wyndham a Buy as I see scope for double-digit earnings growth over the next few years and moderate valuation multiple expansion.

data.title: Centerra Gold: Robust Free Cash Flow In Sight To Enhance Shareholder Value (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Centerra Gold Inc. Is In Full Swing – Excited Participants Take Seat

This analysis positively assesses the growth prospects for NYSE-listed Centerra Gold Inc. (CGAU) shares, more positively than in our previous article from April 25, 2025, where we issued a Hold recommendation.

An Update: We Give Extra Scores to the Positive Outlook of Centerra

We now feel we can issue an update: We share the market's view that this stock is a practical means to profit from the expected further increase in the price of gold. Since our last article on this strong gold producer stock (Centerra is also active in the copper sector and molybdenum sector, but gold is the primary commodity), NYSE shares have risen visibly by 118.85%, representing a total return of 122.91% (including a 4.06% dividend yield). This beat the S&P 500, the US stock index, by 23.70% over the same period. The price of gold (XAUUSD:CUR) rose by 30.7% over the same period. This increase was driven by demand for safe-haven assets, central bank purchases, and hopes for an interest rate cut by the US Federal Reserve. CGAU amplified this increase 3.9 times. This means that, on average, an investment in CGAU generates a return almost four times higher than gold when the gold market is trending upwards. We share the prevailing Wall Street view, which is a Buy recommendation, and are therefore adding scores to our previous outlook assessment for Centerra Gold/CGAU. Thanks to an accelerated, successful, self-financed, and disciplined growth strategy—presented in this article as a solid foundation for long-term, future-oriented growth—CGAU also creates value for its shareholders through dividends and share buybacks.

Our Previous Coverage

Investors holding Centerra shares were considered well-positioned, as the company's impressive liquidity—over $1 billion in cash (including undrawn credit facility) and strong free cash flow—guaranteed continued dividend payments and increased payout potential through share buybacks. This may have dampened the hopes of potential lenders seeking to expand their financing to this well-progressing producer of gold (primarily), copper, and molybdenum as well. The stock is ideal for laid-back investors who do not wait for the word or judgment of the banking world, as the approval of loans to companies in a business cycle like the current one, which is characterised by a credit crunch, acts as a kind of filter between credible and potentially unsuccessful investments. In our previous article, we highlighted CGAU's compelling ability to generate the necessary funds for future growth projects and shareholder returns, largely organically, supported by gold and copper proceeds. It's not common for a gold and base metals producer to pay a dividend, and in the case of CGAU, the quarterly dividend payouts of CAD $0.07 per share (or approximately US$0.051 per share at the time of this article) are anything but modest. Despite the capital-intensive nature of its metal mining and exploration operations, and even with the company renewing its wish to buy back its own shares, there's no sign of debt financing picking up at the end of the tunnel; on the contrary, it's stagnant and low. Seeking Alpha indicates that Centerra Gold's total debt was $20.1 million in the fourth quarter of 2024, $19.5 million in the first quarter of 2025, $19.6 million in the second quarter of 2025, and $17.6 million in the third quarter of 2025. Not only has Centerra not incurred significant debt in recent quarters, but the cash flow statements show that Centerra Gold paid down its total debt by $2.2 million in the March 2025 quarter, $1.8 million in the June 2025 quarter, and $2.2 million in the September 2025 quarter, along with the bullish upward trend in the metal price, as the company operated the business and found the resources to drive its growth projects.

Bridge to the Latest Operational and Financial Situation of Centerra

We observed that the business performed well, particularly thanks to the Mount Milligan gold-copper mine in British Columbia, Canada. The Öksüt gold mine in Turkey had resumed operations after the mercury spill in 2022, but continued to produce less gold than its capacity allows. While we did not rule out the possibility of a less cost-effective business than usual, we saw no cause for concern or any existential threat that could cloud the good sentiment surrounding Centerra's business outlook. The impressive upward trend in the gold price and the rising copper price, which “offset” the negative impact of lower production in Turkey, do not give it the recognition it deserves. Prices were too tsunami-like compared to any potential but reasonable downward trend from Turkish assets in their attempt to regain their ideal form. We had confidence in Centerra Gold Inc. as the company seemed to have a clear vision for its growth projects, and also, molybdenum production in Idaho was expected to begin in 2027. We even suggested higher stakes in Centerra if a lower market value were to become apparent due to volatility.

Centerra Gold Inc., Operational and Financial, as of Q3 2025

Centerra Gold released its third-quarter 2025 operating and financial results on October 28, 2025 (“ Q3-2025 Results”). President and CEO Paul Tomory commented:

This quarter, Centerra sustained robust margins and generated nearly $100 million in free cash flow, driven by strong operational performance at Öksüt and elevated metal prices. Our cash balance increased to $562 million in the quarter, demonstrating our ability to fund the Thompson Creek restart project while returning $32 million of capital to shareholders through share buybacks and a quarterly dividend. We also continued to deploy capital strategically through our equity investment in Liberty Gold Corp., reflecting our balanced approach to growth and value creation.

The financial trends outlined above demonstrate Centerra's continued growth in revenue and profits. With key projects progressing very well towards completion, a disciplined capital allocation provides more room for operating cash flow to be converted into free cash flow. This enables growth in shareholder value, and value creation enthusiasts will be very happy. The value returned in the form of dividends and buyback programs is well received by the US stock market and is paying off in the form of augmented confidence in Centerra's shares, and the momentum being built strongly argues for Centerra's higher market value on the NYSE. As the table of operating results clearly shows, the metal price plays a crucial role in the aforementioned remarkable profit growth. As expected – and the figures confirm our assessments – costs have increased to bring the Turkish operations back to optimal levels. Gold is by far the most important metal in Centerra's consolidated business, with gold sales accounting for 67% of total sales, while copper sales are only 13% and molybdenum sales are around 20%.

Growth Initiatives: An Update

In our previous analysis, readers can find detailed information on each of Centerra Gold's growth initiatives, which Paul Tomory provides an update on in his commentary on Centerra Gold's Q3 2025 published results:

Our self-funded growth strategy continues to advance across multiple fronts. In September, we published the Mount Milligan Pre-Feasibility Study results, which outlined a 10-year mine life extension to 2045 with a disciplined, fully funded $186 million growth capital plan, most of which will not be required until the early-to-mid-2030s. We also expect to publish a Preliminary Economic Assessment for Kemess in the first quarter of 2026. Together, these assets form a robust pipeline of long-life gold and copper projects in British Columbia, while our recently announced Goldfield project in Nevada, provides additional exposure to future gold production. Each of these growth opportunities, as well as the Thompson Creek re-start project in Idaho, can be funded using our existing liquidity and cash flow from operations, positioning Centerra to deliver sustainable, low-risk growth while maintaining our strategic approach to capital allocation.

Mount Milligan Pre-Feasibility Study

Mount Milligan's annual production is projected to reach 150,000 ounces of gold and 69 million pounds of copper between 2026 and 2042. Following this, the facility will process low-grade tailings until 2045. A $36 million upgrade is planned for 2029, and a further $114 million expansion is planned for 2032/33.

Regarding Robust Economics, the company states:

Robust Economics: The PFS reaffirms Mount Milligan’s solid economics, supported by consistent production, disciplined capital investment and low all-in sustaining costs (“AISC”) on a by-product basisNG.

Mount Milligan’s after-tax NPV (5%) (“NPV5%”) is approximately $1.5 billion, based on long-term gold and copper price assumptions of $2,600 per ounce and $4.30 per pound, respectively.

At spot commodity prices of approximately $3,500 per ounce of gold and $4.50 per pound of copper, the after-tax NPV5% increases to approximately $2.1 billion.

Kemess Upgraded Mineral Resource

Updated mineral resource at Kemess, on 11,400 meters of drilling campaign in 2024, estimated as follows:

Kemess's existing infrastructure mitigates execution risk, and the project is expected to deliver 250,000 ounces of gold equivalent over a 15-year period.

Goldfield Project

The Goldfield project is a seven-year gold production project with peak production of 100,000 ounces between 2029 and 2032. The project is economically very robust and achieves an internal rate of return (“IRR”) of 30% at a gold price of US$2,500 per ounce. Given the current gold price of US$4,431 per ounce, the continued optimistic forecasts from analysts for future gold prices (see next section), and the hedging contracts guaranteeing a gold price between US$3,200 and US$4,705 for 2029–2030, the upside potential is enormous. Initial capital expenditures for mine construction are estimated at US$252 million; the company states that it will finance this amount from its own liquidity resources. Production is expected to commence by the end of 2028.

The Molybdenum Operation in Thompson Creek, Idaho

Thompson Creek in Idaho will produce 146 million pounds of molybdenum within 12 years, from the second half of 2027.

The Short-Term Outlook: The Catalyst To Watch

Scenario: Centerra is experiencing a period of strong business growth. Production is expected to be in the second half of the forecast range. Logically, costs will likely tend towards the lower end of the forecast range, as fixed operating costs are spread across a larger projected base of metal units.

The company operates efficiently. It uses capital in a disciplined manner, as it shows, which means that higher, more cost-effective production is achieved with capital expenditure tending to beginning of the spectrum.

These production costs and expenses must be viewed in the context of metal prices, and these prices are expected to be highly favorable to generate solid free cash flow, analysts predict (see next section).

The Gold and Copper, and Moly Price Outlook

Rania Gule, senior market analyst at XS.com, expects “an underlying bullish trend.” for the gold price in 2026, according to Yahoo Finance and a previous article by Yahoo Finance based on gold price forecasts from leading analysts contains:

Paul Williams, managing director at gold bullion supplier Solomon Global, along with Bank of America and HSBC, anticipates a price of $5,000 per ounce by the end of 2026. Goldman Sachs forecasts $4,900 per ounce.

Copper futures (HG1:COM) are currently trading at $5.43 per pound (approximately $11,971 per ton), a gain of 36.31% so far this year. The optimistic long-term copper demand outlook is based on its widespread use in electrical installations and renewable energy generation facilities. The tight supply, on the other hand, is due to production outages at several major mines. A recent copper price forecast comes from UBS and was disclosed last month. The analyst justifies raising his price targets for June and September 2026 to US$12,000/ton and US$12,500/ton, respectively, with a copper shortage in the market and, according to Seeking Alpha, has set a new price target of US$13,000/ton for December 2026.

An internet search for molybdenum price forecasts for 2026 revealed that, generally, analysts continue to expect rising molybdenum prices. This is because the limited supply cannot meet the strong demand, even if a potential global economic slowdown were to dampen demand. This suggests that Centerra's molybdenum business could benefit from a price of at least $24.40 per pound – the price achieved in the third quarter of 2025.

Centerra Gold Inc.'s Market Value

Centerra Gold Inc. shares, listed on the NYSE under the symbol “CGAU”, are trading at $15.06 per share and have a market capitalisation of $2.93 billion at the time of this writing.

Technical Analysis

The current share price is not so far from the upper limit of the 52-week range of $5.41 to $15.47 per share. The RSI of 75.38 indicates overbought territory. Buying now risks missing a more favourable entry point, as the stock first needs to establish a more solid foundation to enable renewed, stronger growth than ever before. Based on the trends signalled by the 20-day moving average (20-MA), the 50-day moving average, and the 200-day moving average, the price development for Centerra Gold is characterised by a persistent upward trend in the short and long term, from which the holdings will benefit.

Valuation

CGAU stock offers a Yield (“FWD”) of 1.40% as of this writing, according to Seeking Alpha's analysts. Dividend Yield (“TTM”) for CGAU is 1.37% versus the Sector Median's 1.87%. The share price of this stock would have to fall by approximately $4.03 per share, or 26.8%, for the dividend yield to match the sector median. Given the current trends in metal prices, as well as Centerra's positive production and growth initiatives, it is too risky to bet on such a lower stock price. A 10% technical correction is already something that is on the verge of dying out. Perhaps it is easier to accept that as a risk for those who already own CGAU than for those who need to start from scratch to build a position in this fast-growing stock. For those who wish to feel more confident in their decision to enter the market at this price level, a comparison of CGAU stock with the sector median and CGAU stock with its own value, represented by a 5-year average based on these financial indicators, could be helpful:

For example, although Centerra's market valuation is very high based on its position in the stock price cycle, 52-week range and RSI, its non-GAAP (“FWD”) P/E is currently over 50% below Centerra's 5-year average. This could also mean that analysts have overestimated future earnings, and if that were the case, and Centerra disappoints in terms of future earnings, the market valuation could drop significantly to lower levels because the stock would appear disproportionately valued by the market compared to its earnings. Because profitability, as described above, is very much dependent on rising metal prices, and because analysts do not shy away from predicting explosive gains in gold and copper prices despite the increases already achieved by them so far – and frankly, their predictions are not unfounded, but solidly rooted in current economic and geopolitical circumstances – it is difficult for us to imagine that analysts have overestimated Centerra’s future profits. After all, it is not a company that has been flawless operationally; it has just recovered from a mercury spill in Turkey that forced very low production capacity for an extended period before Centerra obtained all the necessary permits to resume mining at a more normalized pace. So, we believe that analysts may have relied heavily on metal prices in formulating their estimates of Centerra Gold Inc.’s future profits. If they are wrong, the gold and price forecasts of analysts at major US banks will also be wrong, and in that case, an entire system of estimates would collapse, not just Centerra’s future profits. And frankly, this scenario seems unlikely, as we should assume something other than the macroeconomic and geopolitical positive catalysts that, as we mentioned earlier, outline the reality of this world at the moment. EV/EBITDA (“FWD”), another valuation metric heavily weighted by investors in capital-intensive sectors like gold and copper mining, is 48.09% above Centerra's five-year average.

While this might lead some readers to jump to conclusions and think it contradicts our earlier argument, which revolved around CGAU's non-GAAP P/E (“FWD”) being well below its historical average, we argue it reinforces our thesis that Centerra's earnings are greatly relieved from the burden of interest expense. Indeed, the company will finance with organically generated capital and utilise its credit line, which was not yet drawn at the end of the third quarter of 2025, in a disciplined and prudent manner—no longer at the pace dictated by the alarming debt levels leading up to 2021. In this case, it's a kind of ode to the impending influx of abundant cash flow.

The Risk Section

  • The Sprott Mining Risk Map shows that Mount Milligan in British Columbia, the Kemess Project in British Columbia, Goldfield in Nevada, and the molybdenum mine in Idaho pose a minimal risk for onshore mining and exploration. The Sprott Risk Map also shows that the Öksüt Gold Mine in Turkey poses a moderate risk for onshore mining and exploration.
  • The average trading volume of CGAU shares (“over three months”) of 1.60 million indicates low liquidity (see Seeking Alpha under “ Trading Data”, as of today). One drawback is that investors who need to reduce their positions for personal reasons may not find sufficient demand due to the low daily trading volume. This could mean they have to significantly lower their selling price to sell shares, potentially jeopardizing the success of the trade. On the other hand, the low daily trading volume also creates an environment where upward pressure on the share price can increase dramatically even in response to minor but impactful macroeconomic and geopolitical shifts—from which gold and copper are generally not immune.
  • The biggest risk probably lies in the future price development of gold and copper, especially gold, as this is the biggest driver of Centerra's revenue. Although analysts expect a higher price per ounce going forward, there is always a risk that, for example, a ceasefire in Ukraine could have quite an impact on the gold price – especially if gold has been used extensively in recent months as a hedge against the economic consequences of the Ukraine crisis. This risk is currently mitigated by the situation in the Caribbean, where the US is threatening to prepare for military action and is imposing sanctions on oil tankers from Maduro-led Venezuela. Furthermore, the US Federal Reserve ("Fed") is expected to cut interest rates in 2026 due to falling inflation and a weakening labour market, exacerbating recession fears. But thanks to slogans like “Mine Baby Mine”, the record gold price of 2025 triggered a massive gold boom that perhaps has led to a gold surplus. In 2026, gold prices could fall similarly to oil prices (see the section “Energy break from oil” in this Yahoo Finance article), which could hurt gold mining and exploration companies. The theory of a potential gold oversupply will lower analysts' price forecasts.
  • The liquidity risk is low: In its earnings report for the third quarter of 2025, the company stated the following:

Cash and cash equivalents: As at September 30, 2025, total liquidity was $961.8 million, comprised of a cash balance of $561.8 million and $400.0 million available under an undrawn corporate credit facility.

Let's take the figures from the 2025 capex forecast and conservatively assume that the AISC co-product after 2025 will be US$1,833/ounce (“as of Q3 2025”) in the following 12-month period and that annualized consolidated production will amount to 477,785 ounces of gold equivalent, based on current copper futures at US$5.43 per pound and the gold spot price of US$4,431 per ounce and molybdenum December 2025 price of US$21.3 per pound as well as gold production (“ounces”) of 81,773 and copper production (“thousand pounds”) of 13,354 and molybdenum roasted (“thousand pounds”) of 4,428 in Q3 2025. With an annual gold equivalent of 477,785 ounces and AISCs of US$1,833 per ounce, the total amount is US$875.8 million. This is supplemented by non-sustainable capital expenditures of US$165 million (US$20 million for gold/copper plus US$145 million for the Thompson Creek Mine), current Income tax and BC mineral tax expense and other expenses of US$103 million (US$95 million for gold/copper plus US$8 million for the maintenance of smaller projects).

This is resulting in total costs of US$1,144 million or US$2,394/ounce. Given current and expected future gold prices, Centerra's liquidity is secure.

Conclusion

Even with a slight slowdown in production and potentially marginally higher costs, Centerra Gold Inc.'s NYSE-listed shares would benefit from the enormous price increase in gold (primarily) and copper, which would significantly boost free cash flow. Centerra's free cash flow generation mechanism is so effective that the company can maintain its line of credit untouched and use internally generated cash flow for mineral projects, dividend payments, and share buybacks. Share price gains are likely to continue, especially now that workers in Turkey have returned to work following last year's mercury spill incident, and the market welcomes shareholder appreciation. This is a sign of the investment's real value.

data.title: TQQQ: Replacing QQQ With 3x Leverage Proven To Yield Positive Results
data.description:
Overview

Traditional advice tells long-term investors to buy an index fund and chill. Let the market do its thing and remain patient, and anyone can build wealth with an incremental amount of capital consistently invested over a long period of time. This strategy is typically applied to traditional index-tracking ETFs, such as the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY) or the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ), since they both track an underlying index and offer diversity.

But what if you don't want to wait several decades to see your wealth compound? What if you were ultra bullish on an index and believe that it will be worth more in the future, no matter what challenges or volatility lies ahead? These are the questions that cause investors to take on more risk in the pursuit of accelerating the process. This concept is what the ProShares UltraPro QQQ ETF (TQQQ) reminds me of. TQQQ offers leveraged exposure to the Nasdaq-100 index, with the goal of providing 3x the daily returns.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that TQQQ has delivered a total return of 32%, including all dividends paid. This is an excellent return over a short time span and would make any bullish investor feel like a genius. However, TQQQ may not be the most straightforward buy-and-hold fund since the 3x leverage also applies to any downside risks. So I will be pulling some relevant examples to demonstrate the negative impacts of TQQQ's strategy during different market conditions.

So if traditional advice tells us to dollar cost average into an index fund for decades, is it a suitable approach to dollar cost average into a triple leveraged ETF that tracks the same index? Since the math always assumes that the indexes will trend higher over time, the straightforward answer is yes! However, the process of buying and holding a fund like TQQQ may not be as simple as you think. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that TQQQ implements to generate its returns.

Fund Strategy

TQQQ has an inception dating back to February of 2010, which means we have over 15 years of operating history to reference. The fund has a higher than usual expense ratio of 0.82% due to its more complex operating strategy. According to the summary prospectus, TQQQ seeks to provide 3x the daily results of the Nasdaq-100 index, before fees and expenses. In order to simplify the process that TQQQ implements for readers, I read through this prospectus and will attempt to lay out its structure in order of operations.

The fund achieves its 3x results by implementing leverage but the caveat is that the leverage is reset every single day. This leverage is achieved through the use of swap agreements and future contracts on the Nasdaq-100. The structure is as follows:

  1. For every $100 you invest in TQQQ, it owns $300 worth of exposure to the index.
  2. It achieves the additional $200 difference from debt. This is where the swap agreements and futures contracts come into play.
  3. Let's assume the index rises 10%. ($300 x 10% = $330 value)
  4. $330 value - $200 debt = $130. So TQQQ has achieved its 3x performance goal for the investor.
  5. The fund needs to reset its leverage every day because now look at your numbers. (Current investment $330 / $130 = ~2.5x). This indicates that the leverage shrank. Therefore, leverage needs to be reset daily to keep the 3x objective aligned.
  6. To do this, TQQQ will borrow more cash by the end of the market close, which keeps the cycle going.

Since TQQQ is essentially made up of constituents of the Nasdaq-100, the breakdown by sector is nearly identical. As we can see below, Technology companies make up the largest portion of the fund at 54.66%. This is followed by an allocation to Communication and Consumer Cyclical investments, which accounts for 16.47% and 13.30% of assets respectively. So TQQQ only makes sense if you are an investor that is bullish on the outlook of technology.

Since TQQQ is nearly a copy of QQQ, the holdings also look similar. As we can see below, TQQQ's largest holding is Nvidia (NVDA) at 5.99% of assets. This is followed by exposure to many other notable companies such as Apple (AAPL), Microsoft (MSFT), Broadcom (AVGO), and Amazon (AMZN), just to name a few. However, you'll also notice that a Nasdaq-100 Index Swap and U.S. Treasury Bills as part of TQQQ's top holdings. The fund is top heavy with its top ten holdings making up more than 40% of total assets. While this can be beneficial during bull markets, it can also significantly increase the overall risk profile.

I've issued several articles that highlight my bullish outlook on the S&P 500 and Nasdaq-100 over the next twelve months. The short summary is that I believe the expansion of AI will be the main driving force for many companies. As companies adapt and utilize AI as part of their process, many businesses will significantly reduce costs and this can lead to much healthier operating margins. Eventually, these refinements can flow to earnings growth over time. For instance, the size of the AI market is anticipated to increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 30.6% through 2033.

However, I think it's very crucial for investors to understand how the fund actually works through different market cycles. TQQQ doesn't automatically guarantee a 3x return through all market conditions. The fund can experience erosion in some capacity and may significantly increase the risk for long-term investors. Ultimately, market indexes remain near their all-time highs so it's extremely tricky to suggest buying here.

On one end of the spectrum, I would prefer to wait until there is a large pullback before accumulating more shares. However, I simultaneously believe that the elevated valuation multiples across the technology sector are warranted to an extent. I don't think that investors should let anchoring bias stop them from accumulating shares if they are ultimately bullish on the outlook of the market. On a basic level, the market is always breaking new highs and investors that accumulated at prior highs are happy now, right?

Performance Insights

When looking at the performance over a long period of time, investors may be blinded by the sheer potential of a triple leveraged fund like TQQQ. For instance, we can see below that TQQQ's total return sits around 2.29K% over the last ten year period. This is amazing long-term results, but the chart also indicates there were massive price swings over the last decade. So implementing a long-term buy-and-hold approach requires the ability to stomach large swings in your capital over time.

Even when comparing the performance against QQQ, we can see how significant the outperformance is. Amazingly, TQQQ has actually delivered a return that is more than 3x compared to the index. As we can see below, QQQ's total return sits around 496.5% over the last decade.

QQQ's total return of 496.5% x 3 = ~1,489%

So how does this happen? Well, let's refer to the earlier concept that I laid out, which describes how the fund must reset its leverage on a daily basis. During periods of strong bull markets, this actually works in an investor's favor and creates an effect I call positive leveraged compounding. At the end of every day, TQQQ rebalances and buys more of the index exposure, so when there are periods of consecutive increases, TQQQ is positively compounding your gains through a strong bull market. It's sort of another example of the concept of having your interest earn interest.

The most recent example of this concept was demonstrated over the course of 2025. Markets experienced a very sharp downturn in late March and early April in response to President Trump's tariff proposals. QQQ saw a large pullback but TQQQ's leverage approach caused a pullback that approached ~60% at its lowest point. When the market started to rally over the rest of the year, this positive compound effect took place and rapidly caused TQQQ to eventually surpass QQQ in total return. However, you may notice something when looking at the performance metrics below.

QQQ's total return of 22.3% x 3 = 66.9%

TQQQ only provided a total return of ~40%, which is not 3x.

Risks

The main reason that TQQQ may not perfectly achieve its 3x results over a specific time frame, is due to something called volatility decay. With a traditional ETF like QQQ, a 20% decline requires a 25% gain to recover from the fall. With a triple leveraged ETF, the downside movements are also compounded so it requires massive movements to recover. However, TQQQ will decline 60% if QQQ declines 20%. So in order to get back to positive territory, TQQQ needs to achieve a 150% gain to break even!

Remember, TQQQ needs to reset its leverage on a daily basis. So when it does this during a decline, the fund is essentially buying high and selling low, which compounds losses an contributes to the decay concept. To help visualize this concept, lets imagine:

  1. You invest $100 in TQQQ, so the fund holds $300 worth of value.
  2. The index declines 10% over the year (30% for TQQQ), so your invested capital is worth $70 but the equity the fund holds is worth $270.
  3. TQQQ would now be holding leverage equivalent to 3.85x ($270/$70 = ~3.85x) which means TQQQ is now over leveraged.
  4. In order to get back to the 3x level, TQQQ sells $60 worth of assets, which now values your position at $210. ($210/$70 = 3x)

Another great example of the negative impacts of TQQQ was experienced over the course of 2022. Market indices remained in a downtrend for the entire year as the tech sector pulled back from its highs, uncertainty related to the pandemic remained, and inflationary data was at record highs. As we can see below, QQQ saw a loss of 32.58%, while TQQQ experienced a massive downturn of over 79% for the same time frame.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that TQQQ can be an efficient long-term holding if you truly understand the risks of the fund. TQQQ is most efficiently utilized through strong market rallies, but can lead to disastrous results if we ever see a scenario where the Nasdaq-100 experiences a severe downturn. As we've seen in the past, downturns can cause the fund to experience severe volatility decay due to the effect of negative compounding of its leverage. Therefore, I am rating TQQQ as a buy at this time, despite markets remaining at their all-time highs. The caveat is that I think a dollar cost average is the best approach while at the highs, rather than lump sum investing. I plan to slowly accumulate shares and will wait for a large downturn before I aggressively add large chunks of capital at a time.

data.title: PPG Industries: Poor Performance In 2025, But Likely Better In 2026
data.description:
PPG Industries (PPG) was a company that I was out of for several years, same as the same reason I didn't invest in European giants like AkzoNobel (AKZOY). However, just as I have entered a position in AkzoNobel, I have entered and am increasing my position in PPG Industries.

This company has seen a tradition of multi-year overvaluation, peaking post-COVID-19 in 2020 when it traded at close to 30x P/E relative to a normalized average of about 17-18x P/E (20-year average). While the 1-year return since my last article, found here, might imply to you that this company is not going anywhere, I argue that the underperformance is only a result of the company's further undervaluation, preparing for a reversal backed up by both a recovery in results, as well as a recovery in the market valuation for the company.

Generally speaking, both PPG and AkzoNobel are very solid companies, with their products being sold across two different main markets. If you look at the company's longer-term growth averages, these companies both manage between 5-8% annualized growth - solid and deserving of at least a 15x-17x P/E average, especially considering that PPG Industries is BBB+ rated, and yields well over 2.7% at a price approaching $100/share, a price that in turn implies a P/E discount of below 15x.

The paints and coatings industries and sectors have seen some challenges this year, resulting in some of the declines we're seeing for PPG, but I believe the signs are strong for a turnaround here.

It's based on this earnings turnaround that I believe PPG will be a very solid value-oriented investment, and why I decided some weeks ago to increase my position in the company.

In this article, I'll show you why PPG is becoming one of my favorite investments in the sector, even if you don't - like me - trade in a currency that is currently extremely favorable towards the USD.

PPG Industries - Upside is there, and it's becoming clearer.

I've made it clear in previous articles that because my main trading currency is the SEK, I have to be cognizant not only of the valuation a company has but also of the FX I'm buying it at. This doesn't just apply to USD, of course, but to every non-native stock. The introduction of IBKR into my portfolio has made this a little better, as IBKR doesn't force me to instantly convert currencies, but also, unfortunately, doesn't offer the flat-taxed advantageous accounts I use in Sweden.

The reason I have been slow on the uptake with US stocks is that for parts of 2024-2025, USD pairings were at their peak. They are now normalizing, and it's not wrong to talk about dollars getting "cheap", which is good news for me. It means that investments like PPG are becoming relatively more attractive here.

It amazes me how quickly, and how clearly, a quality company such as this one can go from excessive overvaluation into clear undervaluation, without broader parts of the markets paying notice - but that's what I like about being a value investor. I get to invest in these companies when they are dirt-cheap.

PPG is the largest producer in the world of paints and coatings. As aforementioned, AkzoNobel, another one of my investments, has sold some of its main assets, creating geographic coverage in NA and other parts of the world.

The main challenge to this sector is that the end-users of the coatings and paints that the company creates are very volatile. These not only include automotive but also construction/infrastructure, aerospace, and others.

What I also like about PPG is that the company is focusing more and more on expansion into emerging regions, which can be seen if we look at where the company has been growing strategically, such as with the Comex M&A.

It's fair to say that the company's $18B in sales revenue grows at a GDP-like rate. This is the reason we're seeing 20-year growth of 5-7% in earnings, and it's also the reason the company is entering emerging markets - they want to get a part of that emerging market growth. As mentioned above, organic growth for the company is very low. In order to effectively grow its top and bottom line, PPG needs to supplement organic growth with M&A growth, which it has been doing. The typical target for PPG is a company with technology that it does not yet have.

Its diverse range of customers means that even during a slowdown, which we've been seeing in automotive, the company's earnings do not drop by that much, and that is indeed true. Across the industry, there have also been trends of consolidation as opposed to fragmentation, which is advantageous to large players like PPG.

Recent moves also include PPG selling its architectural coatings business, which lowers its exposure to the construction, residential, and commercial building industry. While I would personally prefer the company to stay diverse, I understand the streamlining considerations behind the move, and it will lower its exposure, making it more into "performance coatings" than before.

The reason this entire sector has seen declines in profit has to do with a number of factors, but the main trends have absolutely nothing to do with PPG itself. The company has a hard time acting against global economic headwinds such as inflation, interest rates, slowing global construction sectors, increasing costs, and stricter regulations. These are things it needs to handle, and this impacts earnings, as we're seeing with an EPS decline. In addition, in some of the company markets, like India, new entrants are coming to the market with cheaper products or better incentives, which increases competition and pushes pricing.

It's fair to say that most of the companies' closest peers are undervalued. Both PPG and AkzoNobel are cheap, which is why I invest. We also have Masco Corp. (MAS), but this is one of the somewhat smaller players with a lower yield. A company that isn't undervalued is Sherwin-Williams (SHW), which some may argue has better brand recognition.

In the worst-case scenario for PPG, I believe we may not see the same historical growth rates as we've seen the past 20 years due to some of the trends I've mentioned - but at the same time, I believe the company's focus on emerging markets should offer a bit better growth to those earnings, albeit at a somewhat higher risk/reward ratio.

Two things make the company interesting, above all others. First off, its emerging market focus should lend to a reversal of earnings growth in 2026-2027E, together with a recovering macro. Secondly, and perhaps more importantly, the last time PPG traded at below 13.6 P/E was during COVID-19. Before that, we'd have to go back 10 years to post-GFC to find the same sort of valuation levels.

If you had invested at any of those times, you'd have enjoyed rotation opportunities beyond 100%+ RoR in less than a 2-5 year period.

I'm counting on the same one here, even if the generally overvalued market may inhibit the timing here somewhat.

I do not believe that Q3'25 is in any way a hindrance to such a development. Q3 was the third consecutive quarter of sales growth, saw a 20%+ EBITDA margin, and a record level of adjusted EPS. Automotive OEM gains speak of the ongoing turnaround, and the company is buying back significant shares.

The company is a very strong player in terms of fundamentals as well. It has a near-$2B cash balance and less than $5.5B of net debt, coming to about 2x net debt/adjusted EBITDA. Share repurchases have been strong and are continuing - at this valuation, I am on board with that strategy.

Together with AkzoNobel, I believe this company to be in a prime position for investment here.

Let's look at company valuation.

PPG Industries - a fair value of at least $140/share, implying a PT of $115 or above.

The question is, of course, where to price the company. We could give the company a forward P/E valuation of 15x, based on 6-7% EPS growth, but this would discount the company's market-leading position and BBB+ credit rating. I believe 16-18x P/E with a midpoint of 17.6x P/E is conservative enough. Note that the company has market-beating upside even to a P/E of 15x.

So, even if only using a discounted P/E, the upside would be market-beating here. Using a bit of a premium, which I believe to be justified here, we get an upside of at least 26-27% per year, to a total RoR of close to 60% by 2027E.

That is what I am investing in. While my original position was bought at a somewhat less attractive price, I have added another 66%, and may add more in the future.

In updating my fair value, I am giving the company a fair value of $150/share, coming to a forward P/E of about 16.5x, discounted by 15% for volatility and for somewhat slower growth, compared to my fair value of $160/share about a year ago. I'm therefore lowering it slightly and establishing a PT of $125/share or below for a market-beating upside. The "at least $130/share" is if you discount the company even further, which I do not believe is warranted or necessary here.

Alternatives, of course, exist. As aforementioned, AkzoNobel is a company I have a close to a full position in. It has better yield, slightly more volatility, but also carries double-digit EPS growth into 2027E. At the same forward P/E, the company carries a 3-4% higher return, and I get to invest in native EUR, which, all things being equal, is somewhat more attractive to me here.

That's why I filled up my AkzoNobel position before I went for PPG - you could go the opposite route.

I believe the company is attractive enough for investment here.

Risks exist, and are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The bigger risk that I see is the company trading this way in what is essentially an inflated market. While investing at a multiple that is essentially a 20-year low, it doesn't mean that in a downturning market, this company couldn't go down more - it absolutely could.

Beyond this, we obviously have a very volatile set of end markets, and despite significant diversification, the company is not immune to trends in these markets. We can see what happens during a slowdown if we look at the GFC, when the company's margins dropped to single digits, which would be about half of where the markets are now.

Still, this is all to be viewed in the context of broader valuation, which is positive here.

Thesis

PPG Industries is a quality company in chemicals/coatings. It has a solid history, good dividend growth, and very good fundamentals, despite increasing debt due to M&A. As of Q3'25, I believe the overall thesis for the company and upside is further improved, while the macro and the forward upside are slightly less certain.

At current valuations, there's a realistic upside to 2026-2027E based on market normalization in key segments, M&A/Integration synergies, cost savings, and recovering demand. The risk is that these developments take longer or don't materialize, as well as the comparatively inflated state of the market, leading me to discount the company more than before.

I believe the likelihood is high that a company that's been performing well for 20 years will continue to do just that. My thesis, based on this picture, is, therefore, a "BUY" with a fair value of $160/share and a PT of $125/share or above. This is now expecting a lower, 16-17x P/E range, compared to 18.5x in my previous upside. The company would need to drop to double digits before I would consider it "cheap" here.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. Based on this and a valuation target of $160/share, where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me, I give the company a "Buy" rating as of December 2025. The company fulfills almost all of my fundamental criteria for investment.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Rotation Fear Won't Last; Betting On Tech's 2026 Comeback
data.description:
With just one more week left for 2025 for us to savor, I believe many of us should really regard it as another highly successful year, with the S&P 500 (SPX) (SPY) up nearly 19% year to date. The Santa rally couldn't have gotten a more solid boost, as the SPX closed at a new high just before Christmas, setting an opportune moment to welcome Santa's arrival to drive a final rally and start 2026 with a bang.

Thus, I think it's also timely for me to reach into my crystal ball (I hope I've found it), hazarding an intelligent and educated guess into where the market could end up at one year down the road. Yet, I must also forewarn you not to take these 2026 year end forecasts too seriously, and you should also take them with extra doses of salt.

Looking back, it was a year arguably filled with anticipation (at the start), then quickly flipped into trepidation (marked by Liberation Day in April), and then we were treated to the "most hated V-shaped rally" in recent times, as the S&P maintained its upward path, and never quite looked back. While downside volatility did attempt to inundate buying pressure from time to time since April, bears never had the conviction and the staying power to see their thesis through, as the market's upside bias persisted with incredible fervor.

Moving on to 2026, the market is now being bifurcated on several notable fronts, as rotation themes gain credence. The AI rally has stuttered recently, with the exception of some tech leaders like Micron (MU) after its most recent earnings. AI chip design leaders like Nvidia (NVDA), AMD (AMD), and Broadcom (AVGO), are still well off their 2025 highs, as the market clearly amped up the capital reallocation, shifting the narrative from growth to value, and from tech to non-tech, as these undercurrents begin to pick up the pace in this end of the year "window dressing" for 2026.

In addition, software companies (IGV) has had better years to account for. With the big exception of (we all know who) Palantir (PLTR), software peers like Salesforce (CRM), Adobe (ADBE), or even ServiceNow (NOW) are seeing tremendous pessimism being heaped onto their price action, as reticent buyers continue to hold back on their accumulation.

The market's penchant to ramp up their bullish thesis on the AI infrastructure plays have not been followed through to the SaaS value chain. Could we finally get a much needed revival from 2026, as the market deliberates on the prospects for a reversal, or a shift to battered software plays that continue to demonstrate robust operating performance, solid margins, and a still nascent opportunity to monetize AI and non-AI workloads.

While AI has been front and center in driving the market's momentums and sentiments for the past few years since the 2022 bear market bottom, we must be careful in being too quick to judge these SaaS firms from being disrupted by the speed of adoption observed in the AI LLM companies. Their economics have obviously not been proven (still unprofitable and burning cash; albeit gross margins have improved), and the Agentic AI revolution may also have been overhyped for now. These "hiccups" provide the opportunity for the SaaS value chain to settle down, regroup, and then make the necessary adjustments to shift their enterprise strategy and cope with the impetus required to secure their footing and ensure that they retain a seat at the table in this unprecedented AI industrial revolution that's arguably far from being in the late cycle phase.

Nevertheless, it's good to see that the S&P 500 is witnessing healthy rotation from the tech and growth leaders, in lieu of sectors that were less favored previously. Sectors like healthcare (XLV), and materials (XLB) have mounted remarkable comebacks to bolster the advance. In addition, risk-on sectors like consumer discretionary (XLY), financials (XLF), and industrials (XLI) have continued to print new recent highs, helping to maintain the market's ascent amidst the worries that are currently engulfing the tech and growth peers.

Nevertheless, I believe while the market leadership appears to have shifted outside of tech now, the tech-heavy S&P 500 cannot be untethered from the AI trade at its core. While we can still consider the secondary or second order derivative in utilities, real estate, or even in energy infrastructure plays in support of the AI infra buildout, the mainstay of the AI growth story will still be firmly based on the picks and shovels companies in tech, I believe.

As seen above, much of the value goes into tech companies that partake in executing the development and completion of the AI data center. Therefore, it's hard for me to envisage that such value extraction could see a new leader emerge, as we are still early in the multi-trillion dollar investment race to define who really owns the AI crown.

Earnings estimates for the SPX have continued rising through 2026. While intermittent fears of multiples compression have appeared from time to time, the market doesn't seem to be perturbed. I also don't anticipate significant risks of a massive decline, as the Fed is still expected to lean dovish, especially since weak consumer sentiments have persisted through December, as labor market conditions could remain tenuous next year. With a dovish-leaning Fed, I believe the market wouldn't be looking for the exit door yet based on worries about Fed policy uncertainties.

The potential for a melt-up phase also cannot be ruled out, which might also trigger a setup leading into a bigger market peak, or what some may contend as the ultimate bull market top, coinciding with the much feared AI bubble burst narrative. In this case, a dovish Fed could spur looser monetary conditions that could spur investors still sitting on the sidelines to return to equities, especially since they may get a lower ROI from their fixed income investments.

In addition, robust earnings underlining the Magnificent Seven are expected to provide the ballast needed for the market to assume a higher than average multiple, even though I'm skeptical of seeing much higher multiples as sustainable without a much needed market reckoning to shake off excess froth.

The current estimates suggest forward operating earnings per share of about $312.4 for 2026 and $358 for 2027. EPS has consistently been adjusted upwards in 2025, as analysts underestimated the underlying strength of the AI trade, while also assessing for higher than expected risks from Trump's tariff and trade ambiguities.

However, I think it pays to be cautious for us to pave the way for 2026's EPS outlook to be driven even higher than the mid-teens growth that is already baked into 2026's prognosis. Hence, while I contemplate a 5% upside to the current forecasts as plausible, I'm not too keen to pencil in even higher optimism from here. This should also create some upside allowances for analysts to revise further upwards, pointing us to a possible level close to the $328 mark. With that in mind, I'm also assigning a 22.5x multiple for the SPX as my base case. I believe that is a plausible and reasonable basis in view of the earnings accretive thesis, predicated against a more dovish Fed.

And, that leads to a price target close to the 7,380 level, pointing to a possible upside of <7% from the current levels.

data.title: I'm Dreaming Of A Silver Christmas
data.description:
Ten days ago, I wrote an update on my bull thesis on silver (SLV)(SIVR). Back in April, I named silver my top pick of the moment that I was buying hand-over-fist, and since then, it has gone on an incredible run.

Then, in my update ten days ago, I stated why I felt like the bull run had further room to run. Since publishing that article, silver has surged over 16% higher in a very short period of time.

That article elicited a lot of questions and comments from readers, which were quite thought-provoking for me. As a result, in today's article, I'm going to share my updated thoughts on how I'm thinking about investing in silver at over $70 an ounce, including the potential for holding it during a recession, how I think about allocating my silver holdings across physical paper and yield-oriented silver investments, and when I would actually consider selling my position.

Why This Silver Bull Market Looks Different

I expect that silver will very possibly experience a sharp correction at some point in the future. Historically, that has always been the case after a massive run-up in the silver price, and I do not expect this time to be any different.

That being said, there are some important nuances to keep in mind that do make today's bull run different from past ones and that make me more bullish on it over the long term. In particular, I expect these differences to make any silver correction less sharp than it has been in the past and to recover much faster, thereby making it a more suitable long-term buy-and-hold investment than it was previously, when it served as more of a speculative play.

Silver Performance During Recessions

First, let me be clear that silver absolutely can fall during recessions and likely will again in the future. This is because industrial demand for the metal weakens. And while silver does also benefit as a bit of a safe haven investment vehicle, the growing industrial demand for silver makes it more levered to economic cycles than gold (GLD) is. Additionally, liquidity becomes very valuable during a recession, so investors are likely to sell silver before they would sell their gold, due to concerns about falling industrial demand and the fact that it is a bit more of a speculative vehicle.

Moreover, investor psychology is impacted by the fact that during past downturns, silver has suffered; therefore, they may expect it to do so again, which would further accelerate its decline in such scenarios. However, in both 2008 and 2020, which are the last two major economic downturn-related silver declines, silver entered both of those downturn periods with substantial inventories and production capacity. Therefore, once demand fell and was expected to fall further due to weakening economic growth, the price tumbled.

Why This Time Could Be Different

Today, however, we have a multi-year streak of structural silver deficits and major macro tailwinds that are driving growing demand for silver that should continue irrespective of the state of the economy, in particular, growing electrification, which drives strong demand for silver. Additionally, mine supply has stagnated, and primary silver mining projects are in scarce supply, with silver mostly being mined as a byproduct of gold.

Of course, this still does not mean that an economic recession would not lead to a silver decline because it would still cause incremental demand for silver to decline, and investor psychology that would likely prioritize gold and cash over silver during an economic downturn could also lead to a decline in silver as investors sell their positions and speculators may short the metal as a pair trade with gold in a risk-off environment.

However, I see a significant decline over a sustained period of time to be unlikely, given that a supply-demand imbalance would be unlikely in favor of supply, given the macro drivers for additional silver demand and the fact that we have been in a structural silver shortage environment for several years now. Therefore, even if the correction is sharp, it would likely be short-lived, and the ultimate plunge would likely be limited.

Additionally, the long-term but growing concern of a U.S. economic default and broader sovereign deficits and debt crises means that monetary metals like gold and silver are increasingly attractive for investors who are willing to hedge their portfolios against long-term financial crises and/or hyperinflation.
